question_text: Saturn's rings are composed primarily of water ice and rock. A scientist observes that the inner rings orbit faster than the outer rings. Using Kepler's Laws and gravitational principles, explain why orbital speed decreases with distance from Saturn.

options.label: A
options.text: Objects closer to Saturn experience stronger gravitational force, requiring them to move faster to maintain orbit and prevent falling toward Saturn.

options.label: B
options.text: Inner rings are made of lighter material that naturally moves faster than heavier outer ring material.

options.label: C
options.text: Saturn's rotation slows down at greater distances, causing the outer rings to move more slowly.

options.label: D
options.text: Outer rings move slower because they contain more ice and friction slows them down.

explanation: Closer objects experience stronger gravitational pull from Saturn. To balance this and remain in circular orbit, they must move faster (applying centripetal force). This is consistent with Kepler's Laws: orbital period increases and orbital speed decreases with distance from the central body.
